The picture is of Philippus Aureolus Theophrastus Bombastus Paracelsus von Hohenheim -- a name that just begs to be declaimed aloud in a stentorian voice with melodramatic gestures. With that said, have at it!

Okay, in that case you need a High John the Conqueror root and a picture of the thing you have a phobia about. Print the picture on flammable paper. Make a fire -- this used to be done in a wood stove, but a fireplace, a barbecue, or even a metal bowl with an inch of high-proof alcohol in it and a cork mat under it to keep the heat from damaging whatever it's sitting on.

So you light the fire, you take the piece of paper with the image of your phobia on it, you spit on it three times -- just a little saliva in each, so it doesn't get too soaked to burn -- and then you put it in the fire and say, "I burn you to ashes, and my fear burns with you." From then on, the carry the High John root with you. Any time you feel frightened, stroke the root three times with your thumb and say to it, silently or out loud, "High John, please lend me a little of your courage." You'll be fine then.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content: I am not clear on the difference, if any, between ceremonial and ritual magic.  And where does hermetic magic fit in?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: You're dealing with brand names, or at least with overlapping labels. The term "ceremonial magic" has ended up being used, for better or worse, for the whole broad tradition of Hermetic-Cabalistic magic that was assembled in the Renaissance and revived by Eliphas Levi in the 19th century. "Ritual magic" sometimes means that and sometimes means any magic that uses rituals. Hermetic magic is magic that draws on the specific tradition of thought that comes from the Corpus Hermeticum, a set of mystical writings from late Roman Egypt. As often as not, when someone says one of these, they mean all three, and vice versa.

comments.title: Dementia and the Spiritual
comments.content:
Hi JMG
I wonder, given your experience working in Aged Care in the past, do you have a magical/spiritual take on the experience?
I ask, as in the past 2 months or so my Mum's (and all of us family members) life has been turned on it's head, after suffering a stroke.
She is currently in respite, after 7 weeks in hospital.
Her baseline before the event was some decline in cognition, plus a fair degree of short term memory loss, ie a form of early stage dementia.
Now, due to the stroke, this process has been accelerated whereby she now exhibits what is termed "Sundowning" whereby her ability to understand among other things, where she is almost non- existent. She will revert to frequented places of her life as to where she thinks she is.
We would love to be able to take her home, but her sleep pattern has become so disrupted that even now, she wakes at least 3-5 times a night. This is better, however than quite a few occasions of being awake for 35 hours plus in a row!
For people living through this very sad condition, when the end finally comes, do you have any theories about the way they leave their body and go to the next stage(s)?
Do they need extra help and guidance?
Also any help from your wonderful readers about caring for loved ones who are in this situation would be greatly appreciated.

Regards, Helen

replies.title: Re: Dementia and the Spiritual
replies.content:
First, please accept my condolences. That's always a difficult thing to go through.

Your mother will be fine. What's happened is that her mental sheath -- the part of her that exists on the mental plane -- has already drifted out of contact with her lower vehicles. It's basically asleep at this point, and will remain asleep until her physical body dies and her etheric body detaches (that latter is the Second Death). At that point her astral body will come back into contact with the mental sheath and the higher levels of the self, and she'll basically blink awake to find herself already in the afterlife. There are many, many more difficult ways to make the transition.

replies.title: Re: Dementia and the Spiritual
replies.content:
I’m glad to hear that, my mom died from vascular dementia and hardening of arteries.

Helen, seek any and all help you can get. Mom was relatively easy to care for. She didn’t become aggressive, wander off, scream all night, call the police, or try to drive, and she remembered how to use the bathroom to the end, and she still kept two people hopping. It took hours to get her to eat and she had to be kept out of the bathroom because she would often think the toilet was the washing machine. She’d stuff a load in, with soap, and flush. That gets expensive.

replies.title: Re: Dementia and the Spiritual
replies.content:
Hello Anonymous

The care home where she is is a nice little place with just 54 beds.
It's not perfect, but what is without plenty of $s? 
For the past 2+ months we have all been on a "roster" spending as much time with mum as possible, but slowly trying to introduce a few gaps now, so that she can get to know the other residents a bit more.

When she moved to care, two weeks ago we tried staying away initially in the evening and let the carers get her to bed, but she just ended up staying up all night, waiting for one of us to "come and pick her up from he local shopping centre".

Now, I or my sister stay with her in the evening til she is tucked up and sleeping. We are on day 5 of this and so far its working - albeit with the wake ups for toilet that the carers are able to manage.
She still has 3 more weeks of scheduled respite, but from there we will be having to make a decision of home care or permanent residence.

We are lucky here in Australia, that due to Mum and Dad's income and asset levels, she will probably qualify for a government residential subsidy and dad will not have to sell the home, being what is deemed a protected person.

The tales that could be told of these past 8 or 9 weeks!!

At this point my main wish is that she NEVER EVER contracts another U.T.I. ( she had 2 in hospital)
they are an absolute waking nightmare for all concerned.

I do wonder if in some instances, urinary tract infections may be what in the past, where considered possession - they really are horrible, especially for the elderly.

replies.title: Re: Dementia and the Spiritual
replies.content:
Thanks for the swift reply,
To add a little context, during the morning and early afternoon her cognition and reasoning is still somewhat evident, so could it be that her mental sheath has not entirely drifted out of contact, but is doing something of a back and forth?

replies.title: Re: Dementia and the Spiritual
replies.content:
My father is 93 and has been slowly declining losing his memory, strength in his legs.
He is taking some of Schüssler's tissue remedies and a caretaker performs some of Donna Eden's Energy Medicine techniques like meridian flush.
It gives some relief but we got to accept that in the end it will be, well, the end.

Now the thing is, we grow so accustomed to taking care of home and children, providing for our families and fielding a bunch of problems that need immediate response, that even at old age we still have that fight flight freeze reflex jolting us from our chairs whenever a situation arises. We are never at ease.
When old age hits, with our vitality decreased, we become fearful we won't be able to make ends meet. That is poison for brain and body.
Very few people get to be at peace with themselves when they go to the next level.

So the most important piece of advice I can give you is: Do not upset her mind in any way. Make her think she is living in the land of make believe. Let her know every prolblem is being taken care of. Laugh and make her laugh.

comments.title: People with serious mental problems

Sir;

you mentioned a few times that people with serious mental problems should stay away from most magical practices. (Interestingly, Zen masters share that opinion and don't want to have psychotics meditate. Actually, even ordinary middle-of-the-road psychiatrists dont want their patients to meditate.)

My question as someone professionally dealing with psychiatric patients is: While I perfectly see why magic or serious meditation will probably destabilize someone for whom stabilisation and holding together the system of ordinary illusions that others might want to transcent is about the most important goal to achieve---is there, besides a merely practical, medical problem, also something deeper, more meaningful about people suffering from, say, a psychotic disease? replies.title: Re: People with serious mental problems
replies.content: "Bad luck" is a label we use for causes we either don't understand or don't want to talk about. Something as serious as a psychotic condition will have important causes, some of which may well be magical in nature: for example, obsession by a hostile spirit. Just as with any other illness, though, there are a lot of possible causes and differential diagnosis is important. Dion Fortune's book Psychic Self-Defence discusses this issue in some detail -- not surprisingly, as Fortune was a working psychotherapist during the early part of her career.

comments.title: Magical housekeeping
comments.content:
And then this happened yesterday...

The house we bought recently came with a severe...wrinkle in the floor, just as you walk in the front door, that extends the length of the house. Investigating, I discovered that the house is 32' wide with a central beam running down the length of it at 16'. Totally normal way to frame up a floor of that size, but where the builder had made his mistake was in building the main floor walls off to the north of that beam by about three feet without adding any additional support beneath it. In other words, the weight of the main floor walls was bearing down on the floor joists far enough off the support beam below that it had, over 80 years, caused a pretty severe and sudden drop in floor height on that side of the beam. In essence, you feel somewhat like you're walking a balance beam down the middle of our house. Except that it only falls away on the unsupported wall side.

So yesterday, in the sporadic rain, I went to work on correcting this problem with 2 bottle jacks and some stout wood posts. It was slow, nerve-wracking work, listening to the house creak and moan above me as I lifted it a millimeter at a time, resting for several minutes every few lifts to check if windows and doors were still working, and I only fixed the first 8 feet of the issue, from the front door in.

It was a psychologically-important 8 feet though, and the effect was quite magical. I knew from our feng shui studies that the floor was causing us some imbalance, perhaps even vertigo, in one of the most important sectors of our house. Leveling it up induced a noticeable magical effect and we both went to bed feeling really good about it.

I love when stuff like that happens and you know immediately that what you did was good and valuable. Like the time we moved our bed and got relief from symptoms the former bad placement was causing even before sleeping in it. That was cool; this was a similar sensation.

I'm just curious here but is it possible for a house to feel something like "pain" from howling construction errors like this one? Can a house "appreciate" its owner's repairs? We get the sense that our house is as glad we're here as we are to be here, and that sense was amplified after taking all that stress off the floor (and beam). Now I'm itching to do the rest of it. replies.title: Re: Magical housekeeping
replies.content: In a living tree, most of the wood is actually dead -- only the layers between the bark and the wood proper is alive. Wood is therefore very good at providing places for the life force to dwell; that's why wood is the best material for magical wands -- and it's also why wooden houses, wooden ships, and (back in the day) wooden-framed airplanes tended to pick up their own guardian spirits. So, yes, you basically cured your house spirit of a really nasty cramp, and I bet it's very happy with you.

replies.title: Re: Magical housekeeping

Historic building restoration is my trade, and I suggest that you lift the rest of the sagging floor over a longer time period, to allow the changes to equalize through the whole house. A quarter inch per week is what I was taught and follow. The sagging itself occurred over many years, and moving the pieces back to where they belong can put a lot of stress on them, as you heard with the creaking and groaning.

I often feel that buildings have personalities and appreciate thoughtful repairs and renovations. May you and your house have many good years together!

replies.title: Re: female goddesses and human sacrifice
replies.content:
If I can comment on the elephant in the room--
Actually, the entity self-described as YHWH is pretty much all about blood sacrifices. Pretty much from Genesis onward, YHWH is commanding animal sacrifices on a regular basis. He commanded Abram to sacrifice his only son Isaac--but held back Abram's hand from making the cut. When the Israelites got to the promised land, He again commanded them, more often than not, to kill every man, woman and child -- and even the livestock -- of the Caananites.
Within Christian belief, the execution of Jesus by the Romans was a continuation of blood sacrifice taking the form of YHWH incarnating his Son to live a perfect life and then to be blood-sacrificed to expiate the sins of the world.
In this way, blood sacrifice continues to be a part of Christian thought, while at the same time being permanently discontinued after the perfect sacrifice of Christ.

Perhaps Orthodox Jewish Persons reading this will correct me if I say in error that, were the Jewish Temple to be re-established in Jerusalem, blood sacrifices of animals would be resumed.

It leads to questions that are difficult for many, such as--

replies.title: Re: female goddesses and human sacrifice
replies.content:
From a Christian perspective (and I'd LOVE to see a Jewish take), the Bible is a very old book, covering an extremely long stretch of history. Human sacrifice was pretty universal in the ancient world, IIRC. The proto-Jews of Genesis are no exception. One example you left out is the story of Jephthah and his daughter-- which is an instructive one, actually, and you may recognize its pattern from other mythologies: Jephthah promises to sacrifice the first thing that greets him when he arrives home, thinking it'll be a dog or goat or something, it turns out to be his beloved daughter...

Anyway, the scriptures are the long, long story of how a religion-- and a contract with God-- evolves over time. Complete with tons of backsliding, reminders, adopting the habits of neighboring people and then purging them again. Almost all the stories of the prophets consist of: the Israelites have been worshipping their neighbors' gods, so God sends a prophet to remind everyone that they signed an exclusivity agreement.

We see it as a gradual revelation. The story of Abram almost-sacrificing Isaac can be (and often is) read as God revealing that no, human sacrifice isn't something He wants His people to engage in, even if all their neighbors are doing it. By the time you get to Jephthah, that's not a story about a man and his trust in God-- it's a horror story. Like: We don't do that anymore, and it's an abomination.

A lot of the most horrific things you find in the Old Testament are... a window into how things worked in the ancient world. Scripture (surprisingly!) is unsparing: life was brutal. Slaughtering everybody was *how you conquered a city.* Much like the story of Cain and Abel is the story of probably countless interactions between early settled farmers, and nomadic herdsmen. There's much that's fascinating in those stories if you try reading the same one from different perspectives: This is the history of the birth and growing pains of a civilization and a religion. It is a set of distilled truths about the nature of human beings. It is the gradual revelation of the nature of God, on an epic timescale. None of those excludes the others.

replies.title: Re: female goddesses and human sacrifice
replies.content:
This is a really good point, methylethyl. I am an observant Jew (I would not say Orthodox, for various reasons, but I've spent plenty of time in Orthodox environs) and many Jews would agree with you that the Old Testament is unfolding revelation.

Most of the rabbinic commentary on the Old Testament takes this unfolding revelation and tries to form a coherent legal system from it that ensures everyone (men, women, children, slaves, foreigners) is taken care of with humanity and justice. Sometimes it's a stretch. Frequently the rabbis ignore quite blatant parts of the text because they violated the current status quo or habits of thought. It's quite fascinating, from many perspectives.

-Ms. Krieger

Israelites smeared it on the door frames in Egypt to ward off the angel of death...

But it’s always animal blood. Human sacrifice is absolutely forbidden. The annihilation of the Canaanite tribes was about separating Israel from the other nations and their gods (it wasn’t entirely effective, obviously. The local gods continued to be very attractive.)

And then...there’s this verse. What I wondered was if it was really to appease some other god, not YHWH, but the Bible whitewashed it. Or the peace treaty was between YHWH and some other god, and the humans violated it. Contracts were always accompanied by animal sacrifice, too. That’s why we say ‘to cut a contract’. The thing you’re cutting is a cow. Usually. Hmmmm.

-Ms. Krieger

comments.title: Abductions and sleep
comments.content:
My five-year-old son had a curious experience in our backyard last week. Observers noted that he turned suddenly and began walking slowly across the yard away from everyone else as if in a trance, and returned a minute later dazed and confused. He reported (and I have every confidence in his sincerity) that a bright light appeared to him and seemed to be blowing him into it with a wind. He apparently came to his senses before reaching it and says he was a afraid something malevolent might get him, so turned around and walked away. Any ideas what this may have been? My first thought was fairy abduction (as the light seemed to be accompanied by some sort of compelling force) but I couldn't think of any precedence off the top of my head. I made them the protective amulets you've described here before (against malevolent magic), but I wonder if there is anything else you would specifically suggest for this sort of case?

A side question I have relates to sleep (I think you may have said something about this before but I'm unable to find it). Is there something about being halfway between sleeping and waking that opens us up to experiencing other senses or beings? I've heard from a number of people about strange encounters and experiences that seem only to happen in this liminal state.

Thank you!

replies.title: Re: Abductions and sleep
replies.content:
I can't say whether the sort of thing your son experienced occurs all over the world, but I've heard similar stories from other places here in North America. Apparently some Native Americans encountered such things in the past, and they still have lore about them.

The consensus among the Native Americans seems to be (to the very, very limited extent of my own second-hand knowledge) that all such encounters are extremely dangerous: predatory as well as seductive. If you do answer their call and go to (or into) them, your family and friends will never see you again.

So your son had a good awareness of real danger. I am very glad that he was strong enough and canny enough to resist the pull of that bright light and the push of that wind.

And yes, some of the European stories about fairies and similar beings do tell of similar things. But in every European story that I have read or heard told, the "pull" and the "push" comes from other-than-human personal beings, not from weird places or things that seem to act on their own. The American and the European cases may be two different "species" of predator.

In my own quite tentative theory of such things, these places are the Other Places. They are enough like our own world that one can describe them to some extent in human language, but they always seem a bit "off" in comparison to our world--and also they seem to want to lure a person in. They come and go: one of these Other Places may show up in your yard on, say, Tuesday, April 8th, but will be gone the next day and never come back to your yard again.

Experiences of these Other Places are different from experiences of another thing, which may be called High Reality. An experience of High Reality cannot be described, even very very roughly, in any human language. Since your son could describe what he encountered, it wasn't (by definition) High Reality.

As for your second question, the states you mention are called hypnagogic (when falling asleep) and hypnopompic (when waking up). There has been a considerable body of scientific work on them, including a good book by Andreas Mavromatis: Hypnagogia (1991). Yes, I do think that they are very important for some sorts of magic.

replies.title: Re: Abductions and sleep
replies.content:
That does indeed sound like faery abduction (or UFO abduction, which is the same thing in modern drag). Iron is the best thing to use to chase off that type of critter; the iron nail and salt amulet will do a fine job.

As for being half asleep, that's when the mental censor we absorb from our upbringing in a materialist culture dozes off, and you can see what's actually going on around you. You can learn to do the same thing while awake, but it takes practice.

comments.title: Evolution, Reincarnation, and Magic
comments.content:
How do you think evolution and reincarnation fit together? If someone reincarnates as the same species, do you think their desires could influence how the species develops? "It sure would been nice in my previous life to have a slightly longer neck to reach those upper branches," etc. I am not convinced natural selection alone explains the evolution of species.

On a related note, do you think the human species evolved the ability to work magic, or is this something all living beings do from single cell on up?

Thank you.

Chris K.

replies.title: Re: Evolution, Reincarnation, and Magic
replies.content:
Druids had that worked out by the late 18th century -- we were into evolution before Darwin published his theories about it. (Iolo Morganwg's Barddas, all the material in which was written before he died in 1826, covers evolution in some detail.) In Druid teaching, each individual soul separates from the mass of unformed soul-stuff we call the elemental world and is embodied in the simplest form of living thing -- what we'd call a single-celled organism. Life follows life, each time picking up a few more possibilities of perception and action, and the soul moves to more complex bodies one small step at a time, all the way up through the circles of Abred, which are the different kinds of incarnate existence.

Each species of living thing except for the sentient species also has a guardian spirit that overshadows it and guides the souls embodied in that form. Those are called "devas" in some modern traditions, and it's as good a label as any. Once you reach sentience, defined as reflective thought -- the ability to think about your thinking -- you go from a collective guardian of a species to an individual guardian spirit. It's the guardians of the species that adapt the living forms over time, using the raw material of mutation and the stabilizing force of natural selection as their tools.

All sentient beings work magic. You become able to work magic the moment you can recognize the difference between a symbol and what it represents -- another good working definition of sentience.

comments.title: Judging others.
comments.content:
I've been thinking lately that the moral lesson of "don't judge others" is primarily a system to get people to stop thinking and forming impressions about other people, and instead to rely on another's impression or simply inherently believe whatever mask someone chooses to wear and display their self as.

Currently, I believe it is about maturing our discernment so we can judge more wisely for ourselves by allowing more data and complexity into our assessment, but without it preventing us from coming to and making decisions or practical conclusions about another's actions or character in what we choose to do about or respond.

Am I missing something? What is bad about 'judging others' in of itself?

replies.title: Re: Judging others.
replies.content:
"Judge not, lest ye be judged" is an instruction that Jesus gave his followers. If you happen to be a Christian, well, he's the boss, and you should follow his instructions. If you don't happen to be Christian, well, do you follow the commandments of any other religion to which you don't belong? Neither do I.

As with every other human activity, judging can be overdone; it can also be done stupidly, maliciously, or both. That doesn't make it a bad thing -- but there are a lot of people who want you not to judge them, because that makes it much easier for them to manipulate you. When you hear someone getting angry because this or that utterance is "judgmental," in fact, you can take it as given that they've got some kind of agenda they don't want you to notice or take into account.

replies.title: Re: Judging others.
replies.content:
How does "Judge not, lest ye be judged" work, though?

I get the whole 'no true double standard' idea of a lack inherent distinction between our emotional reactions inside ourselves and out into the community...but was that teaching also to mean that somehow others will not form impressions of you if you do not form impressions of them?

replies.title: Re: Judging others.
replies.content: Good question. You'll probably want to ask a thoughtful Christian or two about that, since they have experience with it.

replies.title: Re: Judging others.
replies.content:
Judge not, that ye be not judged.
For with what judgement ye judge, ye shall be judged: and with what measure ye mete, it shall be measured unto you. Matt. 7:1-2


Jesus is speaking here, and the passage continues into the mote in your brother's eye and beam in your own, and follows on after Matt. 6, which includes the Lord's prayer and the lovely verses about the lilies of the field.

Compare to other verses spoken by Jesus about judgement, such as John 7:24 which warns to judge not by appearance but judge by righteous judgement,and Luke 6:37 which is similar but not identical to the Matthew account, and follows directly after the healing on the Sabbath of the man with the withered hand and the calling of the Twelve. The entirety of the Matthew passage and the Luke passage have been bothering Christians for centuries, I mean, "Be ye perfect even as your Father in heaven is perfect"? How are we even supposed to start succeeding at that? "Blessed be the poor in spirit, for they shall inherate the kingdom of heaven." Nobody who isn't poor loves that idea.

The conclusion most often drawn about judgement is to be cautious about judging-if you judge by rumors you can expect to be judged according to the same, so consider what evidence is good evidence, and forgive when asked to (or if you've fallen in with a particular heresy, when you're not asked to and the offender has not repented).

And now that I've planted the seeds for the old Christian tradition of a rousing doctrinal fight, I shall say good night to you!

BoysMom

replies.title: Re: Judging others.
replies.content:
Thoughtful Christian here...the interpretation with which I'm familiar is that you will be judged by the same standard by which you judge others, so be careful. The episode of the woman caught in adultery is an example; Jesus' statement "Let the one among you who is without sin be the first to throw a stone" resulted in everyone leaving, _beginning with the elders_ (who realized most quickly their own failings). Similarly, the words in the Lord's Prayer "forgive us our trespasses/debts as we forgive those who trespass against us/our debtors" is interpreted as the action of forgiving obtaining forgiveness. Think of it as Christian karma, perhaps.

RPC

replies.title: Re: Judging others.
replies.content:
"Judge not, that ye be not judged" is in my experience taken as a statement of fact, not a commandment, by most Christians; it's a restatement/extension of the general tendency of justice to be demanding on everyone, frequently discussed here. (And thus, like the raspberry jam principle, it's not perceived as limited to followers of Christ.) The passage goes on "for with what judgment ye judge, ye shall be judged: and with what measure ye mete, it shall be measured to you again."

I read this--and I'm not reading it atypically--as the often-discussed-here raspberry jam principle. It's not about forming impressions of people; it's about trying to enact justice. If you set and act on a standard for justice, it will rebound on you--so doing so is dangerous, and doing so openly and actively makes it far more effective and far more dangerous. Beyond the fact that justice is inherently uncomfortable and unpleasant a good deal of the time, setting standards for justice yourself means the flaws in your standards get magnified back at you. Judge someone harshly for one bad decision a decade ago, and you should be unsurprised when your past misdeeds come back to haunt you. Let someone who is harming others get away with it while claiming to do justice, and you should be unsurprised when people harm you and nothing bad happens to them.

SamChevre

replies.title: Re: Judging others.
replies.content:
If I may jump in: that quote is not about being judged by others: it is about our relationship with God. He applies the standards we use with others for us, and so if we judge someone for having an affair, while involved in an emotional affair, God will remind us of it. He may be gentle about it, but that really depends on how harsh we are with others. The idea is not to suspend judgement, but rather to avoid making judgments based on sins we are also guilty of.

I hope that helps clarify the concept.

replies.title: Re: Judging others.
replies.content: Many thanks to my thoughtful Christian readers for their responses!

replies.title: Re: Judging others.
replies.content:
For my money, judgement always and everywhere has to be balanced against mercy. Either without the other leads one to do evil, and after a while, to become evil.

One possible interpretation of these sayings of Jesus is that whatever balance you are able to strike between justice and mercy during your own lifetime is the same balance that will be applied to you in whatever comes after your death.

replies.title: Re: On Faerie and Retrogrades
replies.content:
1) I changed my mind based primarily on close reading of the Renaissance literature on the subject -- Rev. Robert Kirk's The Secret Commonwealth is a readily accessible English language source for some of this. That literature argues that the behavior of faeries in relation to the material world makes most sense if they have a body of some subtler form of matter.

2) A retrograde is good for anything that has the word "re-" as the prefix in front of its name; thus a Mercury retrograde is great for re-vising. Jupiter retrograde is a great time to re-new your charitable commitments, to re-visit investment decisions, to re-orient toward your life goals, and so on.

comments.title: Flitting about from idea to idea
comments.content:
One issue I've noticed in myself is that I often have many ideas for new projects but never really get going before finding something that makes it less than ideal and then drifting off to another idea -- the cycle repeats itself again and again.

There are a few projects that have been with me for years -- weightlifting, and regular Buddhist ritual practice. When I was younger, I was very interested in languages; I could spend days on studying Japanese, Latin, and other languages and still have a collection of a range of textbooks. Nowadays, I feel like I'm no longer young (I'm 27) and the pressure to have a source of income not dependent upon an employer occupies my thoughts more than my other interests.

I took a look at my natal chart in hopes that it would shed some light on this tendency. My astrological studies are actually another example of my tendency to drift from idea to idea. Astrology is something I try to study every few months, then reach a plateau and put aside, before resuming it again a few months later. I started off mainly with Hellenistic and other traditional, but found that modern astrology does offer some insights that traditional sources don't.

Anyway, I'm a Taurus Ascendant, with Sun, Mercury, and Mars in Sagittarius in the 8th House. Mercury is also retrograde. The Lord of my Ascendant, Venus, is in Libra in the 6th House while Saturn reigns in the 10th in Aquarius.

I feel like perhaps my tendency to drift has to do with my conjunction of Sun, Mercury, and Mars in Sagittarius -- especially with Mercury, since the ideas for projects I have are usually Mercury-related. It seems like a common theme I've seen in modern astrology is that planets in Sagittarius signify doing things in spurts and focusing more on the "big picture" rather than the details -- which does seem to fit with how I do things.

Anyway, would you happen to have any advice on talismans that could mitigate this tendency, or other things I could do (planetary charity?)?

Alternatively, is there a way I can make this work for me? Maybe by working with people with placements that complement mine.

Thanks

replies.title: Re: Flitting about from idea to idea
replies.content: You've just defined what may be the basic challenge you need to work out in this life. You, and you alone, can decide what to do about it.

replies.title: Re: Flitting about from idea to idea
replies.content:
I have the same tendency, and it is slowly settling into me following through with my actions and not drifting off too soon before I've learned a considerable amount about one. What helped was taking up a daily magical practice for the past 3.5 years and examining in meditation every aspect of why I do that, and what I should do instead. Not very easy, but worthwhile. I'd recommend picking one of the things you want to do and just fully commit to doing that thing a minimum of 15-30 mins every day for years, whether it be magic, astrology, piano, or what-have-you. Meanwhile dabble in other stuff if you like, so long as your main devotion is satisfied daily. Once you've learned how to follow through on one thing, other things get easier.

Again, for me that thing was ceremonial magic, and now the other things I always meant to get good at are beginning to fall into place like bricks, but I suspect anything that requires daily study will do if magic doesn't appeal.
